ungrouped.name: Russian Sage
ungrouped.reasoning: Salvia yangii (formerly Perovskia) and all its cultivars ('Blue Spire', 'Little Spire', 'Denim 'n Lace') bloom the same lavender/violet-blue; no pink or white forms reach trade. Stored blue is honest, if really a blue-violet.

ungrouped.name: Lamb's Ear
ungrouped.reasoning: Stachys byzantina is grown for its silver, felted foliage, not its bloom; the mauve-pink flower spikes are incidental and cultivars like 'Silver Carpet' and 'Big Ears'/'Helene von Stein' barely or never flower. Foliage is uniformly silver-grey with only minor cultivar variation.

ungrouped.name: Lily of the Valley
ungrouped.reasoning: Convallaria majalis is white in all ordinary cultivation; a pink 'Rosea' exists but is a genuine collector rarity a normal shopper never encounters. Stored white is the correct default.

ungrouped.name: Blazing Star
ungrouped.reasoning: Liatris spicata is the familiar purple/magenta, but a widely sold white ('Alba', 'Floristan White') is a real second color in the perennial and cut-flower/bulb trade. Essentially a two-color plant, purple being the default.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: This is a genuine two-color plant, not a broadly multicolored one. Purple/rose-purple (the stored #9b5fc6) is the default; white is the only real second color, sold widely as 'Floristan White'/'Floristan Weiss' and 'Alba' at mainstream retailers and, notably, as corms in spring bulb assortments at big-box stores where white and purple are the two standard picks. The "pink/rose" that some catalogs list is a shade within the rose-purple/magenta range, not a distinct garden-center color, so do not add it as a third. The wider Liatris genus (L. ligulistylis, L. aspera, L. pycnostachya) is uniformly purple/magenta, so this is a purple genus with one commercial white sport, not evidence of a multicolor genus. Also note availability is split by format: potted perennials are often just purple, whereas the white option is most reliably found in the dormant-corm/bulb aisle.

ungrouped.name: Goldenrod
ungrouped.reasoning: Every Solidago species and hybrid ('Fireworks', 'Golden Fleece', 'Little Lemon') blooms yellow to gold; the genus has no other flower color. Stored yellow is the only honest option.

ungrouped.name: Japanese Anemone
ungrouped.reasoning: Anemone hupehensis / x hybrida is sold across pink ('September Charm'), pure white ('Honorine Jobert', 'Whirlwind'), and deep rose ('Pamina'); white-versus-pink is a defining shopper choice. Stored pink is the species default.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: The multi-color claim holds and is well-defended: white ('Honorine Jobert', 'Whirlwind') vs pink ('September Charm', 'Robustissima') is a mainstream, everyday garden-center choice, with deep rose/mauve ('Pamina', 'Prince Henry') as a legitimate third. These are mass-market cultivars, not collector rarities, so 'pink' alone understates it. Important bound: Japanese anemones (A. hupehensis / A. x hybrida) live ONLY on the white-pink-rose/mauve spectrum. Do NOT inflate to the reds, blues, or purples of the broader genus, those belong to A. coronaria (De Caen/St. Brigid poppy anemones) and A. blanda, which are different plants. Single vs double is a separate axis and not a color difference.

ungrouped.name: Penstemon
ungrouped.colorSizeCareNote: Red/orange tubular 'penstemons' (pineleaf P. pinifolius, firecracker P. eatonii/barbatus) are compact, xeric western natives wanting lean, sharply drained, low-water soil, whereas the large-flowered purple/pink European border hybrids (P. hartwegii/gloxinioides types) are bigger, shorter-lived, and want richer, evenly moist soil - so a red versus a purple 'penstemon' can be a genuinely different plant that may deserve its own catalog entry.
ungrouped.reasoning: Penstemon is a large genus spanning red, coral, pink, purple, lavender-blue, and white across species and hybrids, so any single stored color is arbitrary. The red/orange tubular species also differ in mature size and care from the big-flowered purple/pink border hybrids.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed multi-color and mainstream, not rare. Two caveats: (1) "Penstemon" is one catalog entry spanning very different plants — tender-ish large-flowered border/Mexicali hybrids (P. hartwegii/gloxinioides: 'Garnet', 'White Bedder', 'Apple Blossom', 'Firebird') vs hardy species (P. barbatus/eatonii red, P. digitalis white, P. heterophyllus violet-blue), so mature size, care, and hardiness vary by cultivar, not just color. (2) There is no true blue — the "blue" penstemons (heterophyllus 'Electric Blue') are really violet/lavender-blue; label it lavender-blue, not pure blue. The existing magenta/pink is a defensible cultivar color ('Hidcote Pink'/'Garnet') but arbitrary as the sole representative.

ungrouped.name: Globe Thistle
ungrouped.reasoning: Echinops ritro and the common cultivars ('Veitch's Blue', 'Taplow Blue') are all steel-blue; a white 'Arctic Glow'/'Star Frost' exists but is uncommon at ordinary retail. Functionally a blue plant, so stored blue is fair.

ungrouped.name: Salvia (Annual)
ungrouped.reasoning: Salvia splendens bedding series ('Vista', 'Salsa', 'Sizzler') come in red, purple, white, salmon, burgundy, coral, and lavender; iconic scarlet is only the most familiar. A prime pick-your-color bedding annual, so red is a fair default but far from the only color.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed, but with a genus caveat: the genus Salvia as a whole is NOT reliably multi-color (most species like S. farinacea, S. guaranitica, or S. coccinea run one narrow palette), so this verdict holds only because the pinned species is specifically S. splendens (scarlet/bedding sage) — the one mass-market Salvia deliberately bred into a color range. Its dominant seed series ('Salsa', 'Sizzler', 'Vista') are stocked at ordinary garden centers in red, purple/violet, white, salmon, burgundy, and pink, so the stored single "red" is only the most iconic, not the only, color. Two trims to the first pass: "coral/pink" is just pink, and "lavender" is a real but series-specific selection (Sizzler Lavender) that sits at the pale edge of the purple range rather than being a clean seventh distinct color. Red remains a fair default.

ungrouped.name: Climbing Hydrangea
ungrouped.reasoning: Hydrangea petiolaris (anomala subsp. petiolaris) bears only white lacecap flowers and, unlike bigleaf H. macrophylla, does not shift pink/blue with soil pH. Stored white is the only realistic color.

ungrouped.name: Climbing Rose
ungrouped.reasoning: Rosa is the archetypal multi-color genus; climbing roses are bred and sold in red, pink, white, yellow, apricot, coral, lavender and bicolors, and shoppers choose by color first. Pink is a fair common default but not uniquely so.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Rosa is the archetypal multi-color garden genus and climbing types specifically (not just bush/hybrid-tea relatives) are widely sold by color: red ('Blaze', 'Don Juan'), pink ('New Dawn', 'Zephirine Drouhin'), white ('Iceberg Climbing', 'White Dawn'), yellow ('Golden Showers', 'Lady Banks'), apricot/orange ('Compassion', 'Crown Princess Margareta'), and bicolor/striped ('Fourth of July', 'Joseph's Coat', 'Handel'). Two trims from the first pass: (1) 'coral' overlaps the apricot/salmon range rather than being a cleanly distinct offering, so I folded it in; (2) 'lavender/purple' is the weakest claim for climbers specifically — the famous mauve/purple roses ('Rhapsody in Blue', 'Ebb Tide') are shrubs/floribundas, and true purple climbers are uncommon at an ordinary garden center, so I dropped it from the defensible set. Blue is impossible (roses lack delphinidin) and correctly absent. Pink is a fair default but not uniquely so.

ungrouped.name: Rosemary
ungrouped.reasoning: Salvia rosmarinus flowers are near-uniformly pale lavender-blue in cultivation; white ('Albiflorus') and pink forms exist but are collector-rare and essentially never stocked at a big-box nursery. Bought as a culinary herb, so flower color is not a purchase driver (the real habit split, upright vs prostrate, is not color-linked).

ungrouped.name: Thyme
ungrouped.colorSizeCareNote: Thymus splits into two garden plants sold under one name: upright culinary thyme (T. vulgaris), a ~12in shrublet with pale lilac flowers grown for leaves, versus mat-forming creeping thyme (T. serpyllum/praecox), a 2-5in groundcover whose signature magenta/pink/red ('Coccineus') and white ('Albus') color IS the stored value. A magenta thyme is almost certainly the trailing groundcover, a different mature size, habit and use than the upright herb, so it arguably warrants its own catalog entry rather than a color note.
ungrouped.reasoning: The genus spans pale-lilac culinary thyme and creeping thymes offered in magenta, pink, red and white; groundcover buyers genuinely pick creeping thyme by flower color. The stored magenta/pink is the creeping-thyme form.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Multi-color is real but comes entirely from ORNAMENTAL creeping thymes (T. serpyllum / praecox), sold as flowering groundcover chosen by color: white ('Albus'), pink ('Pink Chintz'), magenta ('Coccineus'), and the wild purple/lavender form. Two corrections to the first pass: (1) 'Coccineus', marketed as 'Red Creeping Thyme', is actually a deep magenta-pink, not a true red/crimson, so 'red' overstates it; the stored magenta/pink is in fact the most defensible default. (2) Culinary thyme (T. vulgaris) is near-monochrome pale lilac-pink and grown for foliage, so the confirmation depends on 'Thyme' covering the ornamental creeping thymes garden centers stock.

ungrouped.name: Sage
ungrouped.reasoning: Salvia officinalis flowers are consistently blue-violet; the variation shoppers actually choose is FOLIAGE (purple 'Purpurascens', gold 'Icterina', pink-cream 'Tricolor', broad grey 'Berggarten'), not bloom color. Grown chiefly for its leaves, so flower color is not a driver.

ungrouped.name: Chives
ungrouped.reasoning: Allium schoenoprasum reliably produces mauve-purple pompom umbels; a white 'Album' and deeper-rose 'Forescate' exist but are uncommon and rarely picked. Bought as a culinary herb. (White garlic chives, A. tuberosum, are a separate species, not a color form of this one.)

ungrouped.name: Oregano
ungrouped.reasoning: Origanum ranges from insignificant white-to-pale-pink flowers on culinary oregano/marjoram (O. vulgare, O. majorana) to showy pink-rose bracts on ornamental types ('Kent Beauty', 'Herrenhausen'). Most is bought as a leaf herb, so few shoppers select by flower color; the trailing pink ornamentals differ in size but that is a species/use split more than a color-only one.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Oregano is bought as a culinary/foliage herb, not chosen by flower color — there is no white vs pink vs purple SKU on the herb bench. Its true flowers are tiny and insignificant, spanning a narrow continuous pale range (white to pink to lavender-purple) that the stored #c69fd6 already captures. The first pass's showy "pink-rose" ornamentals ('Kent Beauty', 'Herrenhausen', 'Amethyst Falls') get their color from overlapping BRACTS (modified leaves), not petals — a foliage/bract feature and a species/use split (trailing ornamental vs upright culinary), not a flower-color choice within one plant. Keep the single stored purple; pale pink is the only near-defensible second tone and it is not a genuine commerce axis.

ungrouped.name: Mint
ungrouped.reasoning: Mentha produces small, fairly uniform lilac-to-mauve (occasionally whitish) flower spikes across spearmint and peppermint; grown for foliage flavor and never for bloom color. Cultivar variation is in leaf/scent (chocolate, pineapple/variegated), not flower color.

ungrouped.name: Red Maple
ungrouped.reasoning: Acer rubrum is a shade tree grown for form and brilliant red autumn color; summer foliage is uniformly green (the 'red' refers to its early-spring flowers, samaras and fall color, not a leaf-color choice). Unlike Japanese maples in the same genus, this species has no summer leaf-color variants, so flower color is irrelevant to design use.

ungrouped.name: Sugar Maple
ungrouped.reasoning: Acer saccharum is grown for its canopy and orange-yellow-red fall display; summer foliage is uniformly green with no shopper-facing leaf-color forms. Not a flowering-interest plant.

ungrouped.name: River Birch
ungrouped.reasoning: Betula nigra's ornamental value is its exfoliating salmon-to-cream peeling bark; foliage is plain green in summer and yellow in fall, with no flower-color choice for a shopper. A foliage/bark tree, flowers (catkins) are incidental.

ungrouped.name: Ginkgo
ungrouped.reasoning: Ginkgo is dioecious with inconspicuous, non-showy reproductive structures; it is grown purely for its fan-shaped foliage and a uniform brilliant golden-yellow fall color. There is no flower-color choice; buyers pick male cultivars for form and fall color, not blooms.

ungrouped.name: Tulip Tree
ungrouped.reasoning: Liriodendron tulipifera has one distinctive flower form on every tree: cup-shaped yellow-green ('tulip') petals with an orange basal band. As a very large shade tree the high, sparse flowers are not a purchase driver and there are no color variants; stored green (a yellow-green) understates the orange band but is fair.

ungrouped.name: Weeping Willow
ungrouped.reasoning: Salix babylonica bears only inconspicuous yellow-green catkins and is selected entirely for its weeping form and green foliage. No flower-color range exists and foliage color is uniform green.

ungrouped.name: Honeylocust
ungrouped.reasoning: Gleditsia triacanthos flowers are tiny, greenish and insignificant; it is a foliage/shade tree. Foliage DOES vary by cultivar (golden 'Sunburst', bronze-red 'Ruby Lace'), which is a real garden-center distinction, but flower color is a non-factor and mature size is similar across those forms.

ungrouped.name: Littleleaf Linden
ungrouped.reasoning: Tilia cordata has small fragrant pale-yellow flowers valued for scent and pollinators, not color, and comes in no flower-color forms. It is bought as a uniform-green shade/street tree; foliage color does not vary meaningfully.

ungrouped.name: Joe Pye Weed
ungrouped.reasoning: Eutrochium is uniformly in the mauve / dusty-rose to purplish-pink range across all common cultivars ('Gateway', 'Little Joe', 'Baby Joe'); there are no red/yellow/blue forms and white is a rare specialty selection a big-box shopper never sees. Size differences (tall species vs dwarf selections) are cultivar-driven, not color-driven, so stored magenta/pink is accurate.

ungrouped.name: Butterfly Weed
ungrouped.reasoning: Asclepias tuberosa is classically orange, but 'Hello Yellow' (clear yellow) is widely stocked and red-toned forms and 'Gay Butterflies' mixes (orange/yellow/red) are common, so shoppers genuinely choose color. Orange stays the correct default. Note this is the dry-soil orange species, distinct from pink swamp milkweed (A. incarnata) - do not merge.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: I tried to refute the multi-color claim and could not fully knock it down: orange + yellow survives. Orange is the wild default and yellow 'Hello Yellow' is a genuinely mainstream, mass-market cultivar (Bluestone, White Flower Farm, big-box racks, native plant sales), not a rare collector form, so an ordinary shopper really does choose between orange and clear yellow. That makes it genuinely multi-color. BUT I am dropping "red" from the defensible list. A. tuberosa's natural range only runs from yellow through orange to a red-ORANGE/scarlet tint; there is no reliably-stocked, distinctly RED standalone A. tuberosa. The "red" the first pass cited comes from (a) the 'Gay Butterflies' SEED MIX (Park Seed etc.), where you get a random spread of orange/yellow/red-orange, not a color you can pick, and (b) likely bleed-over from a different species. Two confusion traps to flag: A. curassavica (tropical milkweed / bloodflower, 'Silky Red'/'Silky Scarlet'/'Silky Gold') IS the true red-and-yellow bicolor milkweed - do not import its red onto tuberosa. And A. incarnata (swamp milkweed) is the pink/mauve/white one - the first pass was right not to merge it. So: confirm orange+yellow, demote red to a warm-orange tint present only in mixes, keep orange as the correct default.

ungrouped.name: Bluestar
ungrouped.reasoning: Every cultivated Amsonia bears the same pale steel-blue star flowers; the genus has no other flower color. It is also prized for golden fall foliage (esp. threadleaf A. hubrichtii), but that is a seasonal foliage feature, not a purchase-time flower-color choice.

ungrouped.name: Brunnera
ungrouped.reasoning: Brunnera macrophylla flowers are always forget-me-not blue (the rare white 'Betty Bowring' aside), so flower color is genuinely single. However, modern cultivar selection is driven by silver/variegated FOLIAGE ('Jack Frost', 'Looking Glass', 'Variegata'), not blooms; the high shopper interest is for leaves, and mature size is similar across those foliage forms.

ungrouped.name: Lungwort
ungrouped.reasoning: Pulmonaria is genuinely multi-colored: flowers typically open pink and age to blue/violet on the same plant, and named forms span blue ('Blue Ensign'), violet, coral/red ('Raspberry Splash', 'Redstart'), and white ('Sissinghurst White'). It also has silver-spotted foliage variation, and shoppers actively pick both bloom color and leaf pattern. Stored purple is a fair representative but understates the range.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Tried to refute and could not: Pulmonaria genuinely varies within the genus (not confused with Mertensia/Brunnera), and the cultivars driving the range are mainstream, not collector-only ('Raspberry Splash', 'Sissinghurst White', 'Blue Ensign', 'Trevi Fountain', 'Redstart'). Two honest corrections to the first pass: (1) the listed "red" is really coral/salmon/raspberry, not true red, so I folded it into "coral" rather than defending pure red; (2) Pulmonaria is a Boraginaceae color-change plant whose flowers open pink and age to blue/violet on the SAME plant, so any single stored color inherently understates it, purple is a fair-but-incomplete representative. Note also it is dual-purpose, many buyers choose it for silver-spotted foliage, but flower color remains a genuine labeled selection axis, so multi-color stands.

ungrouped.name: Sneezeweed
ungrouped.reasoning: Helenium autumnale and the common hybrids ('Moerheim Beauty' red, 'Butterpat' yellow, 'Mardi Gras'/'Sahin's Early Flowerer' bicolors) span the warm daisy range of yellow, orange, red, and bronze, and garden centers routinely stock several. Orange is a fair, representative default.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed multi-color, not refuted. The color range is carried by mainstream, widely-stocked cultivars, not rare collector forms: 'Moerheim Beauty' (coppery red), 'Butterpat'/'Kanaria' (yellow), 'Rubinzwerg' (deep red), 'Waltraut'/'Wyndley' (orange-bronze), and 'Mardi Gras'/'Sahin's Early Flowerer' (red-and-yellow bicolors). Two nuances a first pass can miss: (1) every Helenium keeps a prominent brown-bronze central disc/cone regardless of ray color, so all of them read as somewhat two-tone and near-pure yellows still show a dark eye; the varying color is in the ray florets, which is what a gardener actually selects on. (2) Common-name risk: 'sneezeweed'/'sneezewort' can point to the unrelated white Achillea ptarmica, but that does not affect Helenium's own genuine range. Orange is an acceptable representative default, though many popular forms sit in the red/bronze end.

ungrouped.name: Solomon's Seal
ungrouped.reasoning: Polygonatum species (biflorum, odoratum, humile, giant multiflorum) all bear the same pendant white-to-greenish-white bells; there is no color range in cultivation. Foliage variegation exists (P. odoratum 'Variegatum', cream-edged leaves), but that is a leaf trait, not a flower-color choice, and shoppers buy it for the arching form.

ungrouped.name: Virginia Bluebells
ungrouped.reasoning: Mertensia virginica is iconically sky-blue, with pink buds that open blue (a characteristic, not a separate offering). Rare 'Alba' white and 'Rubra' pink forms exist but are collector oddities a normal nursery shopper never encounters, so blue is genuinely the only color in trade.

ungrouped.name: Cardinal Flower
ungrouped.reasoning: The species Lobelia cardinalis is defined by its scarlet spikes (hence the name) and is essentially red in cultivation. Other colors on the shelf belong to different taxa (blue L. siphilitica; pink/purple L. x speciosa hybrids sold under their own names), not to cardinalis itself. Foliage varies (green vs bronze-purple 'Queen Victoria'), but flower color does not.

ungrouped.name: Sea Thrift
ungrouped.reasoning: Armeria maritima is predominantly pink, but white ('Alba', 'Cotton Tail') and deep rose/red ('Splendens', 'Dusseldorf Pride') selections are commonly offered as rock-garden/edging plants, so a shopper does encounter a real pink-white-rose choice. Pink is the fair default. ('Rubrifolia' adds bronze foliage but similar size.)
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed multi-color, but with two honest caveats. (1) Confusion risk: the most colorful thrift display a shopper sees is often the 'Ballerina' series (White/Red/Lilac), which is Armeria PSEUDARMERIA (False/Great Thrift), a different species, not A. maritima. Even excluding it, A. maritima proper still holds up: 'Alba'/'Cotton Tail' = white, and 'Splendens'/'Düsseldorf Pride'/'Bloodstone' = deep rose to near-red, all listed as commonly garden-center-available. (2) The first pass's 'deep rose' and 'rose-red' are effectively one deeper-than-default swatch, so I collapsed them to a single 'rose-red'; the genuine distinct choice a gardener faces is pink (default) vs white vs a deeper rose/red. 'Rubrifolia' is a foliage form (bronze leaves, ordinary pink flowers), correctly not counted as a flower color. Lavender/lilac is sometimes cited but leans toward pseudarmeria territory, so I left it out.

ungrouped.name: Candytuft
ungrouped.reasoning: Perennial Iberis sempervirens is grown for its sheet of pure white bloom; that is the product. Newer selections ('Pink Ice', 'Absolutely Amethyst') flush pale pink/lavender as flowers age, but the plant reads white to a home gardener. (The multicolor pinks/purples belong to the separate annual I. umbellata, not this species.)

ungrouped.name: Dusty Miller
ungrouped.reasoning: Jacobaea maritima (syn. Senecio cineraria) is grown purely for its silver-grey felted foliage; the small yellow summer daisies are insignificant and usually sheared off. Foliage is uniformly silver across cultivars (differing only in leaf lacing, 'Silver Dust' vs 'Cirrus'), so there is no color choice to make.

ungrouped.name: Blue Oat Grass
ungrouped.reasoning: Helictotrichon sempervirens is bought for its steel-blue-grey evergreen foliage mound; the oat-like inflorescences are a single straw/golden-tan and secondary. The stored yellow fairly describes the panicle, but the plant's defining and only real color feature is the uniform blue foliage, not a flower-color range.

ungrouped.name: Basil
ungrouped.reasoning: Ocimum basilicum is a culinary herb grown for leaves and typically kept from flowering. Foliage does vary meaningfully: green sweet/Genovese types vs purple-leaved forms ('Purple Ruffles', 'Dark Opal', 'Amethyst'), so leaf color is a genuine shopper choice even though bloom is irrelevant. Green is the correct default. Size differences (small Greek/globe basil vs large Genovese) track type, not color, so no separate color-driven entry is needed.

ungrouped.name: Parsley
ungrouped.reasoning: Petroselinum crispum is a leaf herb harvested before it blooms; foliage is uniformly green. The only real distinction is form (curly 'crispum' vs flat-leaf Italian 'neapolitanum'), which is a leaf shape, not a color, so there is no flower- or foliage-color range for a shopper to pick.

ungrouped.name: Dill
ungrouped.reasoning: Anethum graveolens carries flat umbels of tiny yellow flowers only, with no color range in the species. It is grown for foliage and seed, so a gardener essentially never selects it by bloom color.

ungrouped.name: Lemon Balm
ungrouped.reasoning: Melissa officinalis is grown for lemon-scented foliage; its flowers are insignificant tiny whitish blooms nobody buys it for. Foliage does vary - golden ('Aurea', 'All Gold') and variegated forms sell alongside plain green - so the green-foliage default is fair but not the only leaf option.

ungrouped.name: Monkshood
ungrouped.reasoning: Aconitum is iconically deep blue-purple (A. napellus, A. carmichaelii), so blue is a fair default, but garden centers also carry white and blue-and-white bicolor (A. x cammarum 'Bicolor'), pink ('Pink Sensation'), and a yellow species (A. lycoctonum). Non-blue forms are genuine but only moderately available.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Multi-color is defensible but narrower than the first pass claimed. Blue/violet-purple is the overwhelming default (A. napellus, A. carmichaelii). The genuinely-available non-blue forms are WHITE (A. napellus 'Album', A. septentrionale 'Ivorine') and the BLUE-AND-WHITE BICOLOR (A. x cammarum 'Bicolor', an RHS AGM cultivar and one of the most widely sold Aconitums) — these are real trade cultivars, not collector rarities. I REJECT two of the first pass's colors: (1) PINK ('Pink Sensation') is a real cultivar but only spottily stocked, not something an ordinary gardener reliably encounters; (2) YELLOW is A. lycoctonum/vulparia (yellow wolfsbane) — a DISTINCT species most shoppers never meet labeled as 'monkshood', so it is the classic genus-vs-relative trap and should not count toward this plant's color range. Note that 'purple' and 'blue' overlap heavily here (many napellus forms read more violet than true blue). This is a genuine flower-color case, not a foliage plant, and the genus is not monochrome in cultivation — but blue dominates and the diversity leans toward proper perennial nurseries over big-box retail.

ungrouped.name: Mullein
ungrouped.reasoning: As the wild species Verbascum thapsus yellow is correct, but ornamental Verbascum sold to home gardeners spans apricot, pink, rose, purple, and white (hybrids like 'Southern Charm', 'Helen Johnson', 'Jackie', plus V. phoeniceum). A soft size correlation exists (the tall silver-leaved yellow mulleins are large biennials while most colored forms are shorter short-lived perennials), but yellow also appears in the short hybrids so color does not cleanly determine size.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed multi-color, and the colored forms are mainstream, not collector rarities: Verbascum 'Southern Charm' (creamy yellow, peach, pink, lavender) is stocked by major perennial nurseries and listed by RHS and Missouri Botanical Garden, and V. phoeniceum seed mixes (violet, rose, pink, white) are sold everywhere as cheap seed, plus named hybrids 'Helen Johnson' (apricot/copper), 'Gainsborough' (soft yellow), 'Cotswold Queen' (amber). Two honest nuances: (1) the bare name 'Mullein' most strictly denotes Verbascum thapsus, the woolly roadside biennial, which really is monochrome yellow, so the polychromy lives in the ornamental hybrids and other species, not in 'true' common mullein; (2) the signature garden-Verbascum palette skews to soft apricot/copper/dusky-rose/buff pastels rather than saturated primaries. Keep yellow (it is real in the hybrids too), do not replace it. Note also the size/lifespan wrinkle: tall silver-leaved yellow mulleins are large biennials while most colored hybrids are shorter short-lived perennials, but yellow appears in the short hybrids too, so color does not cleanly predict size.

ungrouped.name: Pincushion Flower
ungrouped.reasoning: Scabiosa is genuinely multicolor in the trade: lavender-blue ('Butterfly Blue'), pink ('Pink Mist'), white, and deep maroon/burgundy (S. atropurpurea) are all standard offerings. Blue is a fair default and shoppers actively pick color on this popular cottage and cut-flower perennial.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed. Perennial Scabiosa is genuinely multi-color at ordinary garden centers: S. columbaria 'Butterfly Blue' (lavender-blue) and 'Pink Mist' (pink) are among the top-selling perennials everywhere, and S. caucasica adds common white ('Alba'/'Miss Willmott') and blue forms. Blue/lavender is a fair default (the iconic 'Butterfly Blue'). Two honest caveats: (1) the deep maroon/burgundy is the weakest color for THIS category because it comes mostly from S. atropurpurea ('Black Knight'/'Chile Black'), which is normally grown as an ANNUAL, not the perennial this entry is filed under; burgundy survives into perennials only via newer selections (Scoop series, 'Beaujolais Bonnets'). (2) Naming-confusion risk: 'pincushion flower' also names Knautia macedonica (crimson relative) and, quite differently, Leucospermum (the orange/red/yellow protea 'pincushion') — neither applies here since genus is pinned to Scabiosa, but a downstream color-scrape on the common name could pull those in.

ungrouped.name: Gaura
ungrouped.reasoning: Oenothera lindheimeri is sold in two clear streams, pure white ('Whirling Butterflies', 'So White') and rose-pink ('Siskiyou Pink', 'Belleza'), plus white-and-pink bicolors ('Rosy Jane'). Both white and pink are ubiquitous, so pale pink is a fair but not exclusive default.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: The multi-color claim survives skeptical review. Distinct pure-white cultivars ('Whirling Butterflies', 'Sparkle White', 'So White') and distinct rose-pink cultivars ('Siskiyou Pink', 'Crimson Butterflies', the Belleza/dwarf pink series) are BOTH mass-market staples, not collector rarities, so white and rose-pink are genuinely available side by side. Two nuances: (1) Botanical confusion risk - the parent genus Oenothera (evening primrose) is overwhelmingly YELLOW-flowered, but this specific species (long sold as Gaura lindheimeri) has no yellow at all; do not let the genus imply yellow. (2) Gaura flowers naturally open white/pale and age to pink on the same plant, so the stored 'pale pink' is a fair intermediate default rather than wrong, it just is not exclusive. The white-and-pink bicolor 'Rosy Jane' is real but meaningfully less ubiquitous than the two pure streams, so I would treat it as a minor extra, not a core color.

ungrouped.name: False Sunflower
ungrouped.reasoning: Heliopsis helianthoides is essentially golden-yellow; recent orange-red bicolor cultivars ('Burning Hearts', 'Bleeding Hearts') exist but yellow overwhelmingly dominates what shoppers actually see. A variegated-foliage form ('Loraine Sunshine') is sold for its leaves, not flower color.

ungrouped.name: Perennial Sunflower
ungrouped.reasoning: Perennial Helianthus (H. x multiflorus, H. maximiliani, H. salicifolius, etc.) are uniformly golden-yellow. The red, bronze, and mahogany forms belong to the annual H. annuus, a different plant, so this perennial entry is honestly single-color.

ungrouped.name: Spiderwort
ungrouped.colorSizeCareNote: The purple 'spiderwort' many shoppers see (Tradescantia pallida 'Purple Heart', also T. zebrina) is a tender, trailing foliage plant grown as a houseplant or annual, with very different mature size and care from the hardy upright perennial garden spiderwort. A purple/trailing form likely warrants its own catalog entry rather than a color swatch on the perennial.
ungrouped.reasoning: Hardy garden spiderwort (Tradescantia virginiana / x andersoniana) sells in blue, violet-purple, magenta-rose, pink, and white ('Innocence'), so it is clearly multicolor with blue a fair default. Separately the purple trailing Tradescantia (T. pallida 'Purple Heart', T. zebrina) is a tender foliage plant of very different size and care shelved under the same genus and common name.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: This verdict applies ONLY to the hardy garden spiderwort (Tradescantia virginiana / T. x andersoniana, Andersoniana Group), which the "Perennial" category correctly selects. That group is genuinely and commonly sold in blue, violet-purple, magenta/rosy-red, pink, and white via mainstream named cultivars ('Zwanenburg Blue', 'Concord Grape', 'Red Grape', 'Pauline', 'Innocence', 'Osprey') found at ordinary garden centers, so the multi-color claim holds up under skeptical review. Two cautions: (1) genus-confusion risk - the same genus and common name also cover the TENDER FOLIAGE plants (T. pallida 'Purple Heart', T. zebrina, T. fluminensis) grown for leaves with small insignificant flowers; those must never be merged into this perennial entry. (2) Blue is a fair default but borderline - most "blue" spiderworts are actually blue-violet; true clear sky-blue is less common than violet-purple in cultivation, so violet-purple is arguably the more representative primary.

ungrouped.name: Foam Flower
ungrouped.reasoning: Tiarella flowers are white to pale blush-pink only; even the 'pink' selections ('Pink Skyrocket', 'Spring Symphony') read as soft-white foam spikes, so white is fair. It is increasingly bought for its heavily cut, dark-veined foliage, which varies far more than the flower color.

ungrouped.name: Red Hot Poker
ungrouped.reasoning: Kniphofia hybrids and species span the classic red-orange bicolor plus pure yellow ('Nobilis', 'Percy's Pride'), coral, cream ('Ice Queen'), and apricot; the Popsicle series alone sells mango/banana/pineapple/redhot. Orange is a fair default for the archetypal poker.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed multi-color; this is not genus/relative confusion, a monochrome genus, or a foliage plant. The mass-market Terra Nova 'Popsicle' series (Mango=orange, Pineapple=yellow, Redhot=red, Papaya, Creamsicle=cream) is stocked at ordinary big-box garden centers, and 'Bees' Lemon', 'Percy's Pride', 'Ice Queen', and coral/apricot cultivars round out the range; the repo's own extension-sourced intel confirms red/orange/yellow (often two-toned on one spike). Two trims to the first pass: 'green-tinged' (e.g. 'Green Jade', greenish 'Percy's Pride') is a specialty/collector color a typical shopper rarely meets, and 'apricot/peach' perceptually shades into orange/coral rather than being a cleanly distinct offering. Orange is a fair default because the archetypal red-to-yellow bicolor torch reads as orange overall.

ungrouped.name: Toad Lily
ungrouped.reasoning: Common garden Tricyrtis (T. hirta, T. formosana) are lilac-purple spotted on white, but white-flowered forms ('White Towers') and genuinely yellow species (T. flava, T. latifolia) reach better shade-plant nurseries. A shade specialist, so availability is moderate.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Toad lily is a shade specialist, not a big-box garden-center staple, so its color range lives mostly in better/specialist nurseries rather than ordinary garden centers. The signature commercial form (T. hirta, T. formosana, and hybrids like 'Tojen') is a white-to-pale ground heavily spotted purple-maroon, which the stored lilac-purple reads well. The one genuinely-available second color in the general shade-perennial trade is pure white ('White Towers', 'Shirohotogisu', f. albescens), so I keep purple + white. I dropped the first pass's "yellow": it is real botany but belongs to distinct specialist/collector species (T. flava, T. latifolia, T. lasiocarpa, T. macrantha) that ordinary gardeners rarely meet - counting it confuses the mainstream hirta/formosana purples with different species. Also note the spotted pattern means "purple" and "white" partly describe the same flower's spots vs. ground, not two cleanly separate color forms.

ungrouped.name: Turtlehead
ungrouped.reasoning: The ornamental C. lyonii/obliqua ('Hot Lips') are rose-pink, but C. glabra (white turtlehead) is genuinely white and widely sold as a native/pollinator plant. Effectively a two-color genus, pink and white; pink is the fair default for the ornamental form.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: The two colors come from two different species, not one variable species. Pink is the dominant ornamental form (Chelone lyonii 'Hot Lips', C. obliqua) that a typical shopper gets on the perennial shelf; white is C. glabra, sold widely but more through the native/pollinator channel than big-box ornamental shelves. Both are genuine, common, and distinct (white is not a rare collector form), so pink+white is defensible at the genus level. Keep pink as the stored default. Do not confuse Chelone with Physostegia (obedient plant), a separate pink/white/purple genus, when tallying colors.

ungrouped.name: Obedient Plant
ungrouped.reasoning: Physostegia virginiana is sold in rose-pink ('Vivid') and white ('Miss Manners', 'Crown of Snow') - essentially a two-color palette. Pink is the most common and a fair default.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed, not refuted. The repo's own deep-intel (src/garden/intel/deepIntel/obedient-plant.json) describes Physostegia virginiana flowers as "pink to pale lilac (also rose or white)" and names two mainstream, widely sold nativars from the same selection line: 'Miss Manners' (white) and 'Pink Manners' (pink). White is not a rare collector form here; the white 'Miss Manners' / 'Crown of Snow' / 'Summer Snow' selections are among the most commonly recommended obedient plants because they are compact and non-aggressive. So pink and white are both genuinely garden-center common. Two nuances: (1) pale lilac/lavender is a shade within the pink range, not a distinct third color, so I would not add it separately; (2) the alternate name "false dragonhead" flags a historical grouping with Dracocephalum (dragonhead) and it can be visually confused with turtlehead (Chelone) or Salvia, but the pink/white palette is correct for Physostegia itself. Pink is a fair default; adding white completes the honest two-color palette.

ungrouped.name: Ironweed
ungrouped.reasoning: Every commonly grown Vernonia (V. noveboracensis, gigantea, lettermannii) is the same iron/red-purple. A white form (f. albiflora) exists but is a rare collector plant a normal shopper never encounters. Species vary greatly in size but that is unrelated to color.

ungrouped.name: Jacob's Ladder
ungrouped.reasoning: Polemonium caeruleum is dominantly lavender-blue, with white ('Album') and pink-apricot ('Apricot Delight') cultivars in trade. Note several popular cultivars ('Stairway to Heaven', 'Brise d'Anjou') are bought mainly for variegated foliage, with the blue flower secondary. Blue is a fair default.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Blue heavily dominates and is the correct default. The genuinely common, defensible second flower color is white ('Album'), a true P. caeruleum cultivar stocked at ordinary nurseries. Pink/apricot ('Apricot Delight') is real but weaker on two counts: it is largely Polemonium CARNEUM, a different (California) species rather than P. caeruleum (a genus-vs-relative confusion risk), and its availability is specialty/mail-order and spotty (one major supplier lists it unavailable for 2026), not routine garden-center stock. Separately, watch a counting trap: much of the popular Polemonium shelf presence ('Brise d'Anjou', 'Stairway to Heaven', 'Snow and Sapphires', 'Bressingham Purple') is grown for VARIEGATED FOLIAGE and all carry blue flowers, so that variety is foliage, not flower color. Realistically a blue-plus-white plant for the average gardener, with pink/apricot a less-common third.

ungrouped.name: Carnation
ungrouped.reasoning: Dianthus caryophyllus is the archetypal multicolor flower - red, white, pink, yellow, purple, salmon, plus picotee bicolors. Shoppers know it and actively choose color. Pink/red is a reasonable default.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Genuine confirm - this is the textbook multicolor flower and I could not refute it. Bred pigment colors (red, white, pink, yellow, salmon, lavender/purple) plus picotee bicolors are all real, stable, and mass-marketed; shoppers actively pick carnation color. One correction to guard against: blue, green, black, and rainbow carnations are artificially DYED cut stems, and the mauve/blue 'Moondust' line is genetically engineered (Suntory), so none of those should be stored as natural colors. Also note the garden-center 'Dianthus' shelf blends D. caryophyllus with pinks/Sweet William hybrids, but the multicolor claim holds for the species itself. Pink is a fine default; red is equally defensible.

ungrouped.name: Sweet William
ungrouped.reasoning: Dianthus barbatus is a classic cottage plant sold in red, pink, white, and purple with strongly patterned contrasting eyes/picotee bicolors, plus dark-flowered/dark-foliage 'Sooty'/'Nigrescens'. Distinctly multicolor; pink is a fair default.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed multi-color; the claim holds under scrutiny. Sweet William is sold predominantly as mixed-color seed packs and bedding six-packs, and the contrasting-eye/picotee bicolor is the species' hallmark rather than a rare form, so ordinary gardeners routinely encounter several distinct colors. Two cautions: (1) the range is white/pink/red/crimson/purple/magenta and dark maroon only - there is no true blue, yellow, or orange in D. barbatus, so do not overclaim those. (2) 'Maroon/near-black' is the specific named cultivar 'Sooty'/'Nigrescens' (grown partly for dark foliage too); widely available as seed but a specialty single-cultivar purchase, not a generic garden-center color, so I dropped it from the defensible everyday set and note it here. Stored default 'pink' is fair, though red and bicolor are equally iconic. No genus confusion issue: relatives (carnations, pinks) share a similar color range, so no risk of borrowing colors that D. barbatus lacks.

ungrouped.name: Ice Plant
ungrouped.colorSizeCareNote: Yellow 'ice plant' is usually a different species (Delosperma nubigenum / congestum): a much flatter, tighter mat that is notably hardier (roughly USDA zone 4) than the taller, wider-spreading, less cold-hardy magenta-pink D. cooperi. That yellow form arguably deserves its own catalog entry rather than just a color note; the many hybrid-series colors (Wheels of Wonder / Jewel of Desert) share D. cooperi-like habit.
ungrouped.reasoning: Delosperma spans magenta-pink (D. cooperi), yellow (D. nubigenum), white, orange, red, purple and bicolor hybrid series - a popular multicolor groundcover succulent. Pink is a fair default for the classic form, but the yellow form is a genuinely distinct, hardier, flatter species.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed, not refuted. Hardy Delosperma is genuinely sold in many distinct flower colors at ordinary garden centers, driven by two mass-market breeding series (Wheels of Wonder: Golden/yellow, Hot Pink, Violet, Fire/bicolor; Jewel of Desert: Garnet/red, Topaz/yellow, Moonstone/white, Amethyst/purple, Sunstone/orange) plus the popular 'Fire Spinner' orange-magenta bicolor. The classic D. cooperi is magenta/pink (stored pink is a fair default) and yellow is a separate hardier species (D. nubigenum/congestum). Two cautions worth noting, though neither refutes the verdict: (1) 'Ice plant' is an ambiguous common name also used for Lampranthus, Carpobrotus (pigface), and Dorotheanthus (Livingstone daisy) - this verdict applies specifically to Delosperma as tagged; (2) many Delosperma flowers are pattern-heavy (glossy petals with contrasting white/yellow eyes), so 'bicolor' here is a real petal-and-center pattern rather than a solid hue.

ungrouped.name: Hens and Chicks
ungrouped.reasoning: Sempervivum is grown for its rosette foliage; it is monocarpic and flowers only rarely (star-shaped pink flowers) before that rosette dies, so flower color is irrelevant. Foliage varies enormously - green, red, burgundy, purple, blue, bronze, chartreuse, often multi-toned/tipped - and shoppers absolutely pick by rosette color. 'Green foliage' is a fair generic default but understates the range.

ungrouped.name: Creeping Sedum
ungrouped.reasoning: Creeping Sedum is a catch-all for several mat groundcover species with genuinely different flower colors: yellow (S. acre, rupestre, kamtschaticum), white (S. album), and rose-red (S. spurium / Phedimus 'Dragon's Blood'), all common at nurseries. Yellow is a fair default but color-picking is real.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: The multi-color claim survives skeptical review, but with three qualifications. (1) "Creeping Sedum" is a catch-all trade name for several distinct mat-forming species, not one species that color-picks; the color range is real but comes from BUYING DIFFERENT SPECIES, not from one plant offered in a palette. Yellow (S. acre, S. rupestre 'Angelina', S. kamtschaticum), white (S. album, 'Coral Carpet'), and pink-to-rose-red (S. spurium / Phedimus 'John Creech', 'Dragon's Blood', 'Fuldaglut') are all nursery staples, so yellow is a fair default. (2) "Pink" and "red" are not two independent colors: both come from the single S. spurium / Phedimus group as a continuum, with 'Dragon's Blood' / 'Fuldaglut' being the deep rose-red end of the same range 'John Creech' anchors at pink. (3) Many of these are grown chiefly for FOLIAGE ('Angelina', 'Dragon's Blood', 'Ogon'), so a shopper often picks by leaf color, but they do all bloom and the bloom colors genuinely differ. Note also S. spurium and S. kamtschaticum are botanically now Phedimus, though still sold everywhere as Sedum.

ungrouped.name: Rock Cress
ungrouped.reasoning: Aubrieta is bred and sold across a wide alpine palette (violet-purple, rose-pink, red, blue, white) via named cultivars like 'Red Cascade', 'Royal Blue', and 'Whitewell Gem'. Purple is the classic default, but shoppers routinely choose among the colors.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Multi-color holds up under scrutiny: Aubrieta is routinely sold by color at spring garden centers via seed mixes (Cascade/Royal Mix) and named cultivars ('Red Cascade', 'Royal Blue', pink and white 'Snowdrift' forms), so purple is just the default, not the only option. Two honesty flags: (1) The first pass's white example is WRONG. 'Whitewell Gem' is actually a deep purple/reddish-violet cultivar (perennials.com literally calls it 'Purple Rock Cress') named for a place, not the color; white does exist but via other cultivars and is the least common shade. (2) Aubrieta 'red' is really carmine/magenta and 'blue' is violet-blue/lavender, both shades adjacent to the purple base rather than true primary red/blue, though they are marketed and read as distinct. Don't confuse the genus with Arabis (also called 'rock cress', typically white/pink) or Aurinia/basket-of-gold (yellow); the genus specified here, Aubrieta, is itself genuinely multi-color.

ungrouped.name: Basket of Gold
ungrouped.reasoning: Aurinia saxatilis is defined by its golden-yellow spring sheets; only marginal collector cultivars deviate ('Citrina' lemon, 'Dudley Neville' apricot), so a normal shopper always gets gold. The name itself fixes the color.

ungrouped.name: Snow-in-Summer
ungrouped.reasoning: Cerastium tomentosum bears only white flowers over silver foliage; no other flower color exists in cultivation. Even the foliage is uniformly silver-grey, so nothing about the color varies.

ungrouped.name: Goatsbeard
ungrouped.reasoning: All Aruncus bloom creamy white, from the giant A. dioicus to the dwarf A. aethusifolius. The large size difference tracks species, not color, since every form is white, so it is not a color-driven size case.

ungrouped.name: Queen of the Prairie
ungrouped.reasoning: Filipendula rubra (incl. 'Venusta') is consistently rose-pink. The white-flowered Filipendulas are separate species (F. ulmaria meadowsweet, F. vulgaris dropwort) sold under other names, so this species itself is single-color.

ungrouped.name: Globeflower
ungrouped.reasoning: Trollius (x cultorum, chinensis) ranges from lemon-cream ('Alabaster') through gold to clear orange ('Orange Princess', 'Etna'). The palette is warm-toned but yellow versus orange is a real, commonly offered choice at garden centers.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: The whole range is warm-toned and continuous (pale cream/primrose -> lemon yellow -> gold -> clear orange); there is NO red, pink, true white, or any cool color, so "multi-color" is real but narrow. The distinct, defensible choices a home gardener actually makes are yellow (T. x cultorum 'Lemon Queen', T. europaeus), orange (T. chinensis / 'Orange Princess', 'Etna'), and cream/ivory ('Alabaster'). I dropped "gold" as a listed color because it over-splits the same yellow-orange axis. Key confusion to avoid: do not conflate Trollius with Ranunculus (Persian buttercup), a separate Ranunculaceae genus that genuinely comes in the full rainbow, nor with Caltha (marsh marigold), which is yellow-only. Cream forms are slightly less ubiquitous than the standard yellow and orange but still commonly sold.

ungrouped.name: Bergenia
ungrouped.reasoning: Bergenia cultivars span white ('Bressingham White', 'Silberlicht'), soft pink ('Baby Doll'), rose, and deep red-magenta ('Winterglut', 'Bressingham Ruby'). Rose-pink is a fair default, but white and red forms are widely sold; the plant is bought for both flowers and evergreen foliage.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Genuine multi-color case backed by standard, widely-stocked cultivars: white ('Silberlicht', 'Bressingham White'), rose-pink (species B. cordifolia, 'Baby Doll'), and deep magenta-crimson ('Winterglut'/Winter Glow, 'Abendglut', 'Bressingham Ruby'). The first pass's "red" is really deep magenta-crimson, not true scarlet, so I merged it into magenta rather than list it separately. Keep pink as the sensible default. Note Bergenia is a dual-purpose plant bought heavily for its bold evergreen foliage (which bronzes in winter), but the spring flowers are still a real color-varied feature, so it is not a foliage-only trap. Do not confuse with the unrelated tropical 'elephant ear' Colocasia/Alocasia.

ungrouped.name: Ligularia
ungrouped.colorSizeCareNote: Flower color roughly tracks two different plants both sold as 'Ligularia': the orange-yellow daisy types (L. dentata, e.g. 'Britt-Marie Crawford', 'Desdemona') are mounding ~3-4 ft with bold rounded, often dark-purple leaves, whereas the bright-yellow spike types (L. przewalskii / stenocephala 'The Rocket') reach 5-6 ft with deeply cut leaves. The orange-vs-yellow split maps loosely onto a genuinely different mature size and form, so these arguably deserve separate catalog entries rather than one color note.
ungrouped.reasoning: Ligularia flowers run yellow to orange-yellow; the deeper-orange dentata daisies and the bright-yellow rocket spikes are both common in the trade, and that color distinction loosely tracks two very different plant sizes and forms.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Ligularia is a warm-tone-only genus (Asteraceae): every species in cultivation flowers somewhere between clear yellow and orange, with NO white, pink, red, purple, or blue anywhere, so it is nowhere near a true multi-color genus. The multi-color claim rests entirely on one real, commonly-sold split: the "rocket" species (L. przewalskii / L. stenocephala, e.g. 'The Rocket') carry bright/clear yellow spikes, while the dentata cultivars ('Desdemona', 'Othello', 'Britt Marie Crawford') carry large orange to orange-yellow daisies that a gardener genuinely reads as orange. Both are trade staples for shade/bog borders, so a home gardener really does encounter both. I dropped the first pass's "gold" as a color — it is just a shade of the yellow already stored (#f5b400 is itself a golden yellow), not a distinct color. Also note the dark-leaved dentata cultivars are bought largely for their burgundy/bronze FOLIAGE, so flower color is a secondary selling point for much of the genus.

ungrouped.name: Foamy Bells
ungrouped.reasoning: x Heucherella (Heuchera x Tiarella) is grown for foliage, which varies enormously (amber, caramel, red, chartreuse with dark veining: 'Buttered Rum', 'Sweet Tea', 'Stoplight'). The airy flowers are incidental and only white to pale blush, so flower color is irrelevant to buyers, who select intensely on leaf color instead.

ungrouped.name: Anise Hyssop
ungrouped.colorSizeCareNote: The warm-toned 'hummingbird mint' hybrids (orange/coral/pink, from A. aurantiaca / rupestris / cana parentage) are generally shorter, xeric, need sharp drainage, resent winter wet and are less cold-hardy, whereas the blue-purple anise hyssop (A. foeniculum) is a tall, moisture-tolerant, self-seeding zone-4 upright. The warm-color forms arguably deserve their own entry rather than a color note.
ungrouped.reasoning: The genus Agastache spans the cold-hardy blue-purple anise hyssops (A. foeniculum) and the warm-toned hummingbird mints (A. aurantiaca/rupestris/cana hybrids) sold in orange, coral, pink and peach; garden centers stock the whole range and color is a deliberate choice.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Naming caution: "Anise Hyssop" strictly means Agastache foeniculum, which in cultivation is essentially blue-purple only (rare white 'Alabaster'/'Alba'). The warm tones (orange/coral/peach/pink) come from the hummingbird-mint hybrids of A. aurantiaca/cana/rupestris, usually badged "Hummingbird Mint," not "Anise Hyssop." This record is defensibly multi-color ONLY because its botanical field is the bare genus "Agastache," not a foeniculum species entry; the tags say "purple/pollinator/fragrant," which lean toward the foeniculum reading, so there is a mild identity mismatch. If the catalog ever narrows this to A. foeniculum specifically, it should revert to near-monochrome blue-purple. As a genus entry, though, the Kudos/Poquito/Meant-to-Bee/Summerlong series make orange, coral, peach, pink, rose, blue-purple, and white all genuinely bench-common (yellow and red also exist). The first pass listing "blue" and "purple" as separate is slightly redundant (these hybrids are marketed interchangeably as blue and purple), but both labels are legitimate.

ungrouped.name: English Daisy
ungrouped.reasoning: Bellis perennis bedding strains (Tasso, Pomponette, Habanera) are sold as pompom doubles in white, pink, red and red-and-white bicolors; the multicolor mix is the whole point of the plant.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed multi-color, not refuted. Standard bedding series (Tasso, Pomponette, Habanera, Bellissima, Speedstar, Goliath) are all sold in white, pink/rose, red, and red-and-white bicolors, both as mixes and as named single colors, at ordinary garden centers as spring cool-season bedding. Caveats: the "red" is really a deep carmine/rose rather than a true red; yellow is only the central disc, not an offered flower color, so do not count it; the multi-color range belongs to cultivated strains, while the wild species form is white with pink-tipped rays. Botanical-confusion note: "English/lawn daisy" (Bellis perennis) must not be conflated with the white-only oxeye/Shasta daisy (Leucanthemum) - here the stored ID is correctly Bellis. The current single stored value "pink" is a legitimate but incomplete color.

ungrouped.name: Echeveria
ungrouped.reasoning: Echeveria is grown for its succulent rosette, whose color varies enormously (blue-grey, green, lavender, pink, near-black, red-margined, variegated) and is exactly what shoppers pick between; the arching bloom stalks are a minor bell-shaped coral-orange-to-yellow, so the stored 'red' flower color is not what defines the plant.

ungrouped.name: Aloe
ungrouped.reasoning: Aloe flower spikes are typically orange but the genus also blooms red, yellow and coral, and ornamental winter-blooming hybrids are bred and sold for those spikes; note that the ubiquitous Aloe vera is bought for foliage and blooms only a plain yellow when mature.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: This is a genus-level entry (~500-600 species), so the multi-color range comes from different species/cultivars, not one plant sold in a chosen color: coral aloe (A. striata) is coral, torch aloe (A. arborescens) is red-orange, Aloe vera blooms yellow, and winter-blooming ornamental hybrids ('Safari Sunrise', 'Tangerine', 'Blue Elf') span red/orange/coral/yellow bicolors. Availability is climate-gated: flowering ornamental aloes are mainstream at garden centers in warm zones 9-11, but in cold climates the only aloe most people own is Aloe vera, a foliage houseplant that blooms plain yellow only when mature and rarely indoors. Do not confuse with monocarpic Agave or the split-off climbing Aloiampelos (e.g. A. striatula, yellow). Stored 'orange' is the modal color but undersells the genus.

ungrouped.name: Agave
ungrouped.reasoning: Agave is a monocarpic architectural succulent grown entirely for its rosette; it flowers once after many years then dies, so bloom is irrelevant. Foliage ranges from blue-grey to green to yellow/white variegated, which is the real thing shoppers choose between.

ungrouped.name: Bugleweed
ungrouped.reasoning: Ajuga reptans flower spikes are reliably blue-violet; white ('Alba') and pink ('Rosea') forms exist but a normal shopper never encounters them. The variation buyers actually pick is foliage (green, bronze, purple-black, tricolor-variegated cultivars like 'Burgundy Glow' and 'Black Scallop').

ungrouped.name: Wild Ginger
ungrouped.reasoning: Asarum is a shade foliage groundcover; its flowers are small brown-maroon jugs hidden at soil level and insignificant to the gardener. Foliage is usually plain green, though some species (e.g. A. splendens) are silver-mottled, which is the only meaningful color variation.

ungrouped.name: Primrose
ungrouped.reasoning: Primula is one of the most rainbow-colored spring genera in cultivation: garden polyanthus and P. vulgaris/acaulis strains come in yellow, red, pink, purple, blue, white and orange, frequently bicolored. The stored yellow is the botanical wild-type but badly undersells the sold range.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: I tried to refute the multi-color claim and could not. The common garden "primrose" (Primula vulgaris / P. acaulis and the polyanthus group P. x polyantha) is one of the genuinely most rainbow-colored spring bedding plants in ordinary commerce: potted primroses in supermarkets and garden centers every late winter/spring routinely include yellow, white, red, pink, purple, blue, and orange, plus picotee/bicolor eyes. This is real cultivation range, not rare collector forms. Two nuances worth recording: (1) "Primula" is a very large genus, and many of its OTHER species are far more color-limited (P. veris/cowslip yellow; P. denticulata mostly lilac/purple/white; P. japonica candelabra reds/pinks/white; auriculas a separate specialist world) - so the rainbow verdict is specifically the everyday P. vulgaris/acaulis/polyanthus material a home gardener actually buys, which is exactly what this catalog entry represents. (2) Unusually, "blue" here is a genuine flower color (e.g. 'Blue Zebra', Pacific/Crescendo blue strains), not the purple-sold-as-blue you must discount in many genera. The stored single yellow (#f0d44a) is the botanical wild-type of P. vulgaris but badly under-represents the sold range. Grown for flowers, not foliage.

ungrouped.name: Cyclamen
ungrouped.reasoning: Florist's Cyclamen persicum and the hardy C. hederifolium/coum are sold across white, pink, magenta, red, purple and salmon; for the popular potted plant, color is a primary purchase decision.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Multi-color is fully defensible, but the rainbow belongs to florist's/potted Cyclamen persicum (the dominant commercial form, tender perennial often grown as a seasonal houseplant), sold in blocks by color where color is the main purchase driver. The genuinely hardy garden species in this genus, C. hederifolium and C. coum, are much narrower (essentially pink, white, and magenta). "Red" here means a deep crimson-magenta rather than a true scarlet, and picotee/bicolor "Victoria" types are also common. Cyclamen also has ornamental silver-marbled foliage, but it is grown and merchandised for its flowers.

ungrouped.name: Trillium
ungrouped.reasoning: The iconic white Trillium grandiflorum dominates, but the genus includes deep maroon-red species (T. erectum, T. cuneatum, T. sessile) and yellow (T. luteum) sold by native/woodland nurseries; availability is moderate and big-box shoppers mostly see only white, so it is a genus with real range but narrow mainstream exposure.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: The genus genuinely spans distinct colors carried by whole common species, not rare sports: white (T. grandiflorum, aging to pink), deep maroon/red-purple (the sessile "toadshade" group — T. cuneatum, T. sessile, T. recurvatum, plus pedicellate T. erectum), and clear yellow (T. luteum). So multi-color is real and defensible. BUT this is a woodland-native specialist plant, not a mainstream garden-center perennial: an ordinary big-box shopper mostly sees only white grandiflorum, and the maroon/yellow range comes from native-plant, woodland-shade, and fall mail-order rhizome nurseries. Two flags: the sessile toadshades are partly grown for mottled foliage and have stalkless flowers (a different look from the iconic white), and much traded Trillium is slow to propagate. Storing only white is understandable (grandiflorum is the icon) but undersells the genus; if a single color must be kept, white is the safest default.

ungrouped.name: Meadow Rue
ungrouped.reasoning: Thalictrum spans lavender/purple (T. delavayi, T. rochebrunianum), white ('Album' forms), pink (T. aquilegifolium) and clear yellow (T. flavum/lucidum); purple is the most common garden form and a fair default, but real color range exists at nurseries.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: The multi-color range is real but genus-level, not a single-species color series: purple/lavender comes from T. delavayi and T. rochebrunianum, white from 'Album' forms, and yellow from a different species (T. flavum / T. lucidum). Buying one species gets you one color, so a gardener assembles the range by choosing species. I dropped the first pass's "pink" — it derives from T. aquilegifolium, whose flowers are really mauve/lilac/rosy-purple and fold into the purple bucket rather than reading as a distinct pink. Availability is solid at good perennial/mail-order nurseries and better independent garden centers, though not guaranteed on every big-box shelf, and the yellow T. flavum glaucum is partly a foliage plant (still flowers genuinely).

ungrouped.name: Sea Holly
ungrouped.colorSizeCareNote: The silver-white sea holly commonly sold under the same name (Eryngium giganteum, 'Miss Willmott's Ghost') is a taller monocarpic biennial that dies after flowering and self-sows, unlike the perennial steely-blue types (E. planum, E. x zabelii). A silver form can signal a genuinely different plant with different lifespan/size, arguably its own entry.
ungrouped.reasoning: Eryngium is iconic steely blue (E. planum, E. x zabelii 'Big Blue'), but silver-white (E. giganteum) and greenish species (E. yuccifolium, E. agavifolium) are also sold; blue is the right default, yet color range and even lifecycle differ across species.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: The ornamental "color" is the spiny bract-collar and cone, not petals, and the iconic "blue" (E. planum, E. x zabelii 'Big Blue'/'Blue Glitter', E. bourgatii) is really a metallic silver-blue that grades into silver, so blue and silvery-white are closer to two ends of one continuum than fully independent colors. Silver-white is still genuinely in commerce via the well-known E. giganteum 'Miss Willmott's Ghost' and E. planum 'White Glitter', but those skew to specialist perennial nurseries. "Green" is the weakest color: it comes from DIFFERENT species (E. yuccifolium / rattlesnake master, E. agavifolium, E. eburneum) whose heads are greenish-white and which are often grown for architectural form and sold at native/specialist nurseries, not as the blue border perennial. The genus entry also lumps species with different lifecycles (E. giganteum monocarpic/biennial vs E. planum perennial). Net: multi-color across the genus is real, but blue is overwhelmingly the default an ordinary gardener meets at a normal garden center; keep blue primary and treat silver-white and green as species-dependent secondaries rather than shelf-mates.

ungrouped.name: Lady's Mantle
ungrouped.reasoning: Alchemilla mollis produces only frothy chartreuse/yellow-green flower sprays; there is no other flower color in cultivation, so the stored 'green' (chartreuse) is accurate and the sole form.

ungrouped.name: Moonflower
ungrouped.reasoning: Ipomoea alba, the night-blooming moonflower, is essentially always pure white; unlike its multicolored morning-glory relatives (I. purpurea/tricolor), this species has no meaningful color range.

ungrouped.name: Boston Fern
ungrouped.reasoning: Nephrolepis exaltata is a spore-bearing fern grown for bright green fronds; it does not flower and foliage is uniformly green (rare variegated novelties like 'Tiger Fern' aside).

ungrouped.name: Ostrich Fern
ungrouped.reasoning: Matteuccia struthiopteris is grown for its green shuttlecock fronds and edible fiddleheads; non-flowering with a single green foliage color.

ungrouped.name: Japanese Painted Fern
ungrouped.reasoning: Athyrium niponicum var. pictum genuinely varies in frond color across garden-center cultivars: silvery blue-green (the 'teal' default), deep burgundy/wine ('Burgundy Lace', 'Regal Red') and greener 'Ghost'-type hybrids. Non-flowering, but foliage color is a real shopper choice, so 'teal' is only one of several forms.

ungrouped.name: Christmas Fern
ungrouped.reasoning: Polystichum acrostichoides is an evergreen native fern grown for its green fronds; non-flowering with a single green foliage color.

ungrouped.name: Maidenhair Fern
ungrouped.reasoning: Adiantum is grown for delicate green fronds (some species flush coppery-pink when new but mature green); non-flowering and effectively one foliage color.

ungrouped.name: Autumn Fern
ungrouped.reasoning: Dryopteris erythrosora ('erythro-' = red) is named for its coppery-bronze new fronds that mature to green; non-flowering, but its signature seasonal copper flush is the selling point, so stored 'green' captures the mature color but misses what shoppers buy it for.

ungrouped.name: Lady Fern
ungrouped.reasoning: Ferns reproduce by spores and have no flowers; Athyrium filix-femina is grown for its finely divided green fronds. The colorful silver/burgundy 'painted' ferns are the separate species A. niponicum, not this one, so this reads as plain green foliage.

ungrouped.name: Cinnamon Fern
ungrouped.reasoning: No flowers. The 'cinnamon' name refers to the brown fertile spore fronds, not a flower color; the sterile foliage is green. Single foliage color.

ungrouped.name: Royal Fern
ungrouped.reasoning: Spore-bearing fern with no flowers; green fronds. Young growth can flush bronze and 'Purpurascens' has purplish new fronds, but that is a minor foliage-tint variant, not a flower color, and the plant reads green.

ungrouped.name: Holly Fern
ungrouped.reasoning: No flowers; Cyrtomium falcatum is grown for its glossy dark-green, holly-like leathery fronds. Single foliage color.

ungrouped.name: Douglas Fir
ungrouped.colorSizeCareNote: The blue-green Rocky Mountain form (var. glauca) is genuinely hardier (roughly USDA zone 4), slower-growing, and more compact/narrow, while the green coastal form (var. menziesii) is a massive, fast timber tree hardy only to about zone 6. The glaucous-blue foliage tracks that different provenance, so a blue Douglas fir could warrant its own catalog entry rather than a color note.
ungrouped.reasoning: Conifer with cones, not flowers; foliage ranges from green (coastal) to glaucous blue-green (Rocky Mountain var. glauca). It is a large forest tree rather than a typical planting-bed purchase for a non-expert home gardener.

ungrouped.name: Pachysandra
ungrouped.reasoning: Pachysandra terminalis is grown as an evergreen foliage groundcover; its small spring flower spikes are white to greenish-white only. Leaf-variegated forms ('Variegata','Silver Edge') exist, but flower color is genuinely single and buyers do not pick it by bloom color.

ungrouped.name: Periwinkle
ungrouped.reasoning: Vinca minor is famously periwinkle-blue, but garden centers routinely stock white ('Alba') and reddish-purple/burgundy ('Atropurpurea') flowered selections, plus gold/variegated-foliage forms. A real color range, though blue is the dominant default.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed multi-color, but with caveats. (1) Blue/lavender massively dominates; white ('Alba') and wine-purple/burgundy ('Atropurpurea', a.k.a. 'Rubra') are genuine, buyable minorities, not the norm. (2) The 'purple' entry covers a burgundy/violet-purple range, not a clean magenta. (3) Do NOT confuse this with Catharanthus roseus, the annual 'vinca'/Madagascar periwinkle (a different genus, sold in pink/red/white/apricot) — the first pass correctly avoided this trap. (4) The gold and variegated forms ('Illumination', 'Blue and Gold', 'Variegata') are FOLIAGE selections that still bloom blue; they add leaf color, not a flower color, and should not be counted toward flower-color range. Multiple university extensions (Clemson, NC State) list 'Alba' and 'Atropurpurea' as commonly grown cultivars, so this is mainstream availability, not collector-only.

ungrouped.name: Dead Nettle
ungrouped.reasoning: Lamium maculatum is sold in distinct named color forms over its silver-marked foliage: pink/magenta ('Beacon Silver','Pink Pewter'), pure white ('White Nancy'), and purple ('Purple Dragon','Orchid Frost'). Genuinely multi-color and shoppers choose by it.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Lamium maculatum is grown primarily for its silver-marked FOLIAGE, and its flowers are small, so a casual shopper often selects by leaf look rather than bloom. But flower color is still a genuine, named distinction among mainstream cultivars over that similar silver foliage: pink/magenta ('Beacon Silver', 'Pink Pewter', species type), pure white ('White Nancy', one of the most-sold forms), and purple/orchid ('Purple Dragon', 'Orchid Frost' — a mass-market Proven Winners selection). None are rare collector items. Do NOT extend this to yellow: yellow-flowered 'dead nettle' is a DIFFERENT species (Lamium galeobdolon, yellow archangel); L. album (white archangel) and the weed L. purpureum are also separate species and should not be counted as L. maculatum colors.

ungrouped.name: Sweet Woodruff
ungrouped.reasoning: Galium odoratum produces only tiny white star-shaped flowers; there are no other flower-color forms in cultivation. Stored white is honestly the sole color for this species.

ungrouped.name: Barrenwort
ungrouped.colorSizeCareNote: Color roughly tracks a real size/vigor/care split within this genus-level entry: the yellow forms are mostly the vigorous, evergreen, dry-shade spreading groundcovers (E. x perralchicum 'Frohnleiten', E. pinnatum subsp. colchicum, E. x versicolor 'Sulphureum'), whereas the red/pink/white/lavender forms are the daintier, often deciduous, slower clumping species (E. grandiflorum, E. x youngianum, E. x rubrum). The tough yellow groundcover types may deserve their own entry from the clumping colored ones.
ungrouped.reasoning: Epimedium is stored as a genus (no species pinned) and spans many species/hybrids sold in yellow, red, pink, white, orange, and lavender; the stored yellow is only one common form (E. x versicolor 'Sulphureum'). Clearly multi-color across the genus.
ungrouped.verifyCaveat: Confirmed multi-color, but the first pass slightly overstates uniform availability. Because the entry is stored at genus level with no species pinned, the color range is defensible: it maps to distinct, established, widely-grown taxa rather than rare collector forms — yellow (E. x versicolor 'Sulphureum', E. x perralchicum), crimson (E. x rubrum), and the grandiflorum/youngianum pink/white/purple forms are all standard trade plants; orange is real via E. x warleyense. Two honest nuances: (1) at a big-box garden center an ordinary gardener most reliably encounters only yellow (x versicolor) and red (x rubrum); the pinks, whites, purples, and especially orange skew toward independent perennial nurseries and mail-order, so 'commonly available' is true at a decent perennial section but thin at mass retail. (2) Epimedium is grown for its ornamental semi-evergreen foliage as much as its flowers, but flower color is a genuine, cultivar-named selling axis, so it is not a foliage-only case. No genus/relative confusion detected — the candidate colors are not borrowed from a lookalike. The stored single yellow is one legitimate common form but under-represents the plant.

groups.note: All Osteospermum/Dimorphotheca; members span the realistic African daisy range (purple, white, pink, yellow, orange). No true red/blue exists in this plant, so nothing is missing.
Lily of the Nile realistically comes only in blue/violet and white; blue covers the violet shades, so blue+white fully spans the shopper-expected range.
Floss flower is dominantly blue/lavender with pink and white cultivars; blue, pink, white is the complete practical range.

Grouping spans genuinely different ornamental onion forms (giant globe, drumstick, star-of-Persia, yellow moly), not just colors. Purple/pink/white/yellow are well covered; true-blue Allium caeruleum is a real but niche gap.

Members are distinct forms (trailing love-lies-bleeding vs upright plume vs foliage tricolor), not one plant recolored. Red/green/orange present; gold-bronze ('Hot Biscuits') and deep burgundy plume forms are a modest gap.
Hippeastrum in red, white, pink, orange covers the core range; bicolor/striped forms are stylistic rather than a missing base color.

Very different forms grouped (spring wood/blanda, poppy anemone coronaria, tall fall-blooming Japanese anemone). Color coverage is broad (blue, pink, purple, red, white, magenta) so no color gap despite the form spread.
Summer snapdragon in purple, blue, pink, white spans its full realistic palette.
Phlox drummondii in orange, pink, purple, red, white covers the range; lavender/blue tones fall under purple, so no meaningful gap.
Thuja is a foliage conifer with no ornamental flowers; members differ by form/size (columnar, globe, golden). No flower-color range is expected, so the empty color set is correct.

Spans different aster species/forms (New England, alpine, etc.) but a shopper reads them as one daisy. Blue, purple, pink, red, white, magenta cover the range; asters have no true yellow so none is missing.
Similar feathery plumes across chinensis/japonica/Arendsii hybrids; pink, red, white, purple, salmon (orange) span the full false-spirea palette.
Autumn crocus realistically comes in pink/mauve, purple, and white; the trio fully covers the garden range (yellow Colchicum is a rare oddity, not shopper-expected).
Spans evergreen Japanese azaleas and deciduous types (a real form split) plus colors. Coverage is excellent (magenta, orange, pink, purple, red, white, yellow), effectively the full azalea spectrum.
Bachelor's button centered on classic blue, plus pink, purple, red, white (and near-black maroon under red/purple); the range is well covered.
Trailing Sutera in white (classic), blue/lavender, pink, purple spans its realistic range.
Taxodium is a deciduous conifer tree with no ornamental flowers; members are size/form cultivars. Empty color set is correct; no flower color is expected.
Platycodon in blue/purple (classic), pink, and white is the complete realistic range for the species.
Ornamental feature is berries, not flowers; members are different Callicarpa species. Berry color range (vivid purple/magenta plus the white-berried form) is fully covered.
Monarda hybrids in red (classic), pink, purple, magenta, white; lavender falls under purple, so the range is well covered.
Spans very different forms (wax, tuberous, dragon-wing, and foliage rex) plus colors. Orange, pink, red, white, yellow cover the flowering range well; apricot/salmon read as orange.
Historically golden-yellow; modern breeding adds orange, red, pink, white and bicolors, all present here, so the range is fully covered.

Rudbeckia group spans species (annual hirta cultivars like Cherry Brandy/Cherokee Sunset plus perennial fulgida/Goldsturm) but reads as one gold, dark-centered daisy. Yellow/orange/red covers the full warm range; no pink or blue exists for this plant.
Thunbergia alata color forms of one vine. Orange/yellow/red/pink are well covered, but the very common white 'Alba' (dark-eyed) form sold in the Susie/other series is absent.

Blanket-flower cultivars (pulchella/aristata/grandiflora) differ mainly by color and look near-identical. Red/orange/yellow plus their bicolors is the complete warm range; gaillardia has no cool colors, and peach/burgundy fall inside the orange-red gradient.
Combines old-fashioned Lamprocapnos spectabilis with the finer fringed Dicentra eximia/formosa (distinct forms). Pink/white/deep-red is the entire realistic color range for the type.

Foliage grass: Festuca glauca cultivars differing in blue intensity and size. Grown for blue foliage, not bloom; the stored 'yellow' is only the incidental seed head, so flower-color coverage is not a shopper factor here.
Parthenocissus tricuspidata cultivars grown for fall foliage color, not flowers (blooms are insignificant green). No bloom-color range applies to this vine.
Buxus species and forms (sempervirens, microphylla/koreana, dwarf vs columnar hedging shapes). An evergreen structure shrub with no ornamental flower color; color coverage is irrelevant.
Amethyst-flower color forms of one plant. Blue, violet-purple and white is the complete range Browallia offers.
Mostly Buddleja davidii color forms plus the yellow weyeriana type and dwarf hybrids. Purple/pink/magenta/white/blue/yellow spans the genus comprehensively.

Foliage plant; members are leaf-color forms (white, pink, red patterning). Stored flower colors are empty because caladium is grown for its leaves, so the shopper decision is leaf color and n=5 spans the white/pink/red palette.
Pot-marigold color forms. Orange/yellow/cream (pale yellow) is the classic range; the rare pinkish novelties aside, coverage is complete.
Million Bells span nearly the entire spectrum across 8 colors including blue, orange, and yellow. Excellent coverage.
Eschscholzia cultivars of one species. Classic orange plus yellow/cream/pink/purple/red covers the full realistic garden range.
White Zantedeschia aethiopica plus the colored elliottiana/rehmannii hybrids (distinct forms). Eight colors including a near-black ('grey') span the full available range.

Spans japonica vs sasanqua (different species and bloom seasons). White/pink/red is the complete garden range; camellia has no blue or orange, and yellow (C. nitidissima) is a rare collector species only.
Canna x generalis hybrids (with varied bronze/variegated foliage). Red/orange/yellow/pink is the core range; near-white 'Ermine' types exist but are a minor omission, not a shopper frustration.
Campanula medium color forms. Blue/pink/purple/white is the complete range for this biennial.
Nepeta is fundamentally a blue/lavender mounding perennial. Blue dominates, with the minor pink ('Pink Cat') and white forms also present; no other flower colors exist for the plant.

Spans distinct forms (plumose feather, cristata cockscomb, spicata wheat). Warm red/orange/yellow/pink/magenta is celosia's iconic range and is well covered; magenta stands in for the purple 'Intenz' novelties.
Hardy garden-mum cultivars. Yellow/orange/red/pink/purple/white spans the full mum range; there is no true blue mum.

Spans very distinct groups (large-flowered jackmanii-type hybrids, viticella, montana, evergreen armandii). Purple/blue/pink/red/white covers the showy hybrids well, but the yellow species clematis (tangutica/orientalis, e.g. 'Golden Tiara') is a real category that is absent - a minor gap since most shoppers picture the cool-toned hybrids.
