Spider-flower color forms of one plant. Pink/purple/rose(red)/white is the complete ornamental range; garden cleome has no yellow or blue.

A habit group (climbing roses) varied by color. Strong warm/pink/red/yellow spread, but no true white despite white climbers being iconic (Iceberg, New Dawn, Sombreuil); pale pink is the nearest. Lavender/mauve also absent but that is niche. Minor, not notable, since pale pink partly covers and shoppers still have range.

Foliage plant (Coleus/Plectranthus scutellarioides), grown for leaf color, not flowers; its flower spikes are insignificant. Empty flower-color set is correct and expected. The real 'color range' here is foliage (burgundy, lime, red, chartreuse), which this framework does not track. No flower-color gap.
Aquilegia hybrids/species. Blue, purple, red, pink, white, yellow spans essentially the full columbine range including the classic bicolor territory. Well covered.

Echinacea color selections. magenta/pink stands in for the classic purple coneflower, plus orange, red, yellow, white, and the modern greens. Full modern range covered, as expected for this well-bred group.
Foliage plant (Heuchera), bought for leaf color (caramel, purple, lime, silver); flowers are minor. Empty flower-color set is correct and expected. No flower-color gap.
Warm yellow-gold-orange-red range plus newer pinks. That is effectively the whole Coreopsis palette (the genus does not offer blue/purple/true white). Complete for the plant.

Spans two species: C. bipinnatus (pink/white/magenta) and C. sulphureus (orange/yellow/red), which differ in foliage and height, so it is mildly a species mix rather than pure color variants. Color coverage itself is complete for a shopper (chocolate cosmos maroon is the only niche absence).
Flowering crabapple (Malus) cultivars. White, pink, and red/rose is the entire crabapple bloom range; there is no yellow/blue/purple crabapple flower. Complete.
Lagerstroemia color selections. White, pink, red, purple/lavender, magenta is the full crape myrtle range; the genus has no yellow/orange/blue. Complete.
Phlox subulata/stolonifera mat phlox. Pink, purple, blue/lilac, white, red, magenta covers the entire creeping-phlox palette. Complete.
Crocosmia is a warm-only genus: red (Lucifer), orange, and yellow (George Davison) is its entire realistic range. Nothing missing.
Multiple crocus species presented as a color set. Purple, white, yellow, and blue/lilac is the full crocus range (plus striped bicolors). Crocus offer no orange/pink/red. Complete.

This group bundles genuinely distinct plants, not one plant's colors: cigar plant (C. ignea, orange/red), bat-faced cuphea (C. llavea, red/purple), and Mexican heather (C. hyssopifolia, purple/pink/white) are different-looking species. The groupType is the notable thing here; color spread itself is fine.
Narcissus. Yellow, white, orange, cream (pale yellow), and pink-cupped (pale red) covers the whole daffodil range; there is no blue/purple/true red daffodil. Complete.

Large well-bred group. Red, orange, yellow, white, pink, and magenta/pink (covering the purple/lavender end) span the dahlia palette; true blue does not exist in dahlias. Covers color well, as expected.
Hemerocallis. Yellow, orange, red, purple, magenta/pink, and cream/near-white (pale yellow) span the daylily range; true blue/pure white do not exist in the genus. Well covered.
Blue, purple, white, pink, and lavender (magenta/pink) is the classic delphinium palette and its full common range. Yellow/red delphiniums exist only in rare niche species. Complete for a shopper.

Pinks/carnations. Core pink, red, white (plus a coral-orange) are present, but lilac/mauve/purple dianthus are common in garden centers and absent here. Minor, not notable, since the core three colors and picotee bicolors are the main draw.

Bundles distinct Cornus species: flowering/kousa dogwood (white/pink/red bracts), cornelian cherry (C. mas, the yellow), and likely redtwig types (grown for stems). The species mix is the notable feature; the white/pink/red flowering-dogwood color range a shopper expects is fully covered.

Foliage plant grown for large leaves (Colocasia/Alocasia; the group likely spans those genera, so it is mildly a form mix). No meaningful flowers, so the empty flower-color set is correct. No flower-color gap; the real range is leaf color (green, near-black, variegated).
David Austin roses, 32 members. Red, pink, white, yellow, apricot/orange, and the pale/blush tones span the full English-rose palette; there is no blue/purple rose. Excellent coverage.

False indigo. Built on distinct species (B. australis blue, B. alba white, B. sphaerocarpa yellow) plus modern Decadence hybrids, so it is part species-mix, part color range. Blue, purple, yellow, white, pink, red covers the full modern Baptisia palette.
Scaevola aemula color variants. Blue/purple, pink, white is the full realistic fan-flower range; violet is covered by blue. Complete.

Calamagrostis cultivars (e.g. Karl Foerster/Overdam) differ by habit and variegation, not flower color. The single 'yellow' is the golden plume/seedhead. Flower-color range is not a shopper concern for grasses.

Only 3 members (pink/white/yellow) with NO red. Red is the signature rose color and red floribundas are common (Europeana); a shopper wanting one is stuck. Unlike a large rose group, this small set does not cover color.
Distinct Prunus species/forms (Yoshino, Kwanzan, weeping, Okame). Flowering cherries only bloom in white and pink shades, so pale-pink/pink/white is the complete natural range.
Flowering tobacco spans bedding N. alata color forms plus distinct species (green N. langsdorffii, tall white N. sylvestris). Green/white/pink/red/purple covers the range; lime is captured by green.
Cultivars/size-forms (Lynwood Gold, dwarf types) differ by habit, not color. Forsythia is only yellow, so the single color is the complete range.
Pennisetum types: purple-leaved 'Rubrum', pink-plumed 'Fireworks'/orientale, etc. The colors are foliage/plume tones across distinct cultivars, not a flower-color range a shopper picks by.
Mirabilis jalapa color forms. Magenta/orange/pink/red/white/yellow plus the implied bicolors is essentially the full four-o'clock palette.
Digitalis purpurea color forms plus distinct species (yellow D. grandiflora/lutea, rusty-orange D. ferruginea, apricot 'Sutton's'). Purple/pink/white/yellow/orange covers the range well.
Full rainbow (blue, orange, pink, purple, red, white, yellow). Freesia's entire garden color range is represented.

Very distinct plants grouped by genus: Crown Imperial (F. imperialis, tall orange/yellow), snake's-head (F. meleagris, small checkered purple/white), F. persica (deep purple). Different forms rather than colors of one plant; color range is adequately covered.

All members sit in the pink/red family. Fuchsia's signature look is the purple/violet corolla (usually over pink or red sepals), and pure whites are common. No purple in a fuchsia group is a real gap a shopper would notice.
Garden phlox with 13 members across pink, purple, white, magenta, red and coral (orange). Excellent, complete coverage.
Cultivars (August Beauty, Frostproof, Radicans, Kleim's Hardy) differ by size/hardiness/form. Gardenias are only white/cream, so the single color is the complete range.
Orange, yellow, red, pink, white covers the core gazania palette; only niche bronze/mahogany tones are absent, which is negligible.
Zonal Pelargonium. Core colors present (red, pink, white, magenta, purple), but salmon/coral, one of the most popular geranium shades, is missing. Minor since it reads as a pink/red tone.
Orange, yellow, red, pink, white covers the primary gerbera range; only cream/bicolor niceties are absent.
Nine colors including green and pale pink. Full gladiolus spectrum represented.
Globe amaranth in magenta, purple, white, pink, red, orange ('Strawberry Fields'). Complete garden range.
Chionodoxa comes in blue (dominant), pink ('Pink Giant'), and white ('Alba') only, all present. Complete range.
Muscari, mostly M. armeniacum forms (blue, white 'Album', pink) plus the yellow oddball species. Blue-dominant range is well covered; near-black/purple types read as blue.

True cranesbill (perennial Geranium), correctly kept separate from Pelargonium. Blue, purple, pink, magenta, white is the complete hardy-geranium range; they do not come in red/orange/yellow, so nothing is missing.

Rose mallow (Hibiscus moscheutos) cultivars. White/pink/red/magenta is the full hardy-hibiscus range; there is no true yellow, orange, or blue in this species (those are tropical hibiscus). Coverage is complete.

Eight distinct colors (green, white, pink, purple, red, yellow, pale orange/apricot) spans the hellebore palette very well; the dark 'slate/near-black' end is effectively covered by purple. Mostly Helleborus x hybridus with a green species tone, so treated as color variants.
Tsuga conifers (species/weeping/dwarf forms). Grown for evergreen foliage and habit, not flowers, so flower-color range does not apply; empty color set is expected.

Alcea rosea color variants. Pink/purple/red/white/yellow already spans the spectrum; the only common garden color absent is apricot/peach (and the classic near-black, which purple/red approximates). Minor refinement, not a shopper blocker.

Lonicera group spans genuinely different climbing species (trumpet, Japanese, woodbine, coral). Orange/yellow/pink/red/pale-yellow covers the honeysuckle palette well (cream/white via japonica). Distinct forms, but color coverage is fine.

16 distinct Hosta cultivars (blue, gold, green, variegated, giant, mini). A foliage plant; its lavender/white flowers are incidental, so an empty flower-color set is correct and no color gap applies. Real variety here is foliage, not bloom.
Hyacinthus color variants across blue, purple, pink, magenta, white, yellow, red, and salmon/apricot. This is the full hyacinth range; excellent coverage.

Only 5 members spanning pink/red/yellow. Hybrid tea and grandiflora roses are THE multicolor cut-flower rose, and white and orange are among the most iconic, most-shopped rose colors, both absent. A shopper wanting a white or orange rose would be clearly frustrated. Unlike the large well-covered rose groups, this small set genuinely under-covers.

Members span distinct forms (bigleaf mophead vs panicle vs oakleaf/smooth) but the color set is only blue and white. Pink is missing entirely, yet roughly half of bigleaf hydrangeas are pink (pH-dependent blue/pink split) and many panicles age to pink/red. This is the textbook grouped-by-form-but-missing-the-color-split case; a shopper expects pink hydrangeas.

Impatiens color variants (walleriana plus New Guinea forms): magenta/pink, orange, pink, purple, red, white with salmon/coral implied. Impatiens have no true blue or yellow, so this spans the realistic range.

Only 3 members, all blue/purple. Iris means 'rainbow' and bearded/Dutch/Siberian irises cover the entire spectrum; yellow, white, and pink are extremely common and all absent. Members also straddle iris forms (mixed), but the color gap is the real problem: a rainbow flower shown only in blue/purple clearly under-covers.
Cupressus sempervirens columnar-conifer forms/cultivars. Grown for evergreen form; no flower color applies, so the empty set is expected.
Hakonechloa macra foliage forms (green, gold 'Aureola', variegated). An ornamental grass grown for foliage; flower color is not the shopping axis, so the empty color set is correct.

12 distinct Acer palmatum cultivars/forms (upright vs laceleaf, green vs red/purple vs variegated). A foliage tree; its tiny red flowers are not the shopping axis. Real variety is leaf color/form, so an empty flower-color set is expected, no gap.

Distinct Jasminum species (white common/star jasmine, yellow winter jasmine, pink-budded J. polyanthum). White/yellow/pink is the realistic jasmine range; coverage is complete for genuinely different species.
Juniperus conifers spanning groundcover to upright forms and green/blue/gold foliage. Grown for evergreen foliage, not flowers; empty color set is expected and no gap applies.
Lantana camara color variants including its signature multicolor clusters: yellow, orange, red, pink, purple, white. This is the full lantana range; excellent coverage.
Consolida/annual larkspur variants in blue, purple, pink, white. That is the realistic larkspur palette; it does not come in red/orange/yellow, so nothing meaningful is missing.

Lavandula cultivars (with some English/French/Spanish species mixing) in blue, purple, pink, white. Lavender does not come in warm colors, so blue/purple plus pink and white cultivars is the full realistic range.
Syringa color variants: purple, lilac-blue, magenta/pink, white, and even the famous yellow ('Primrose'). Spans the full lilac range including the rare yellow; coverage is complete.

Members span lily forms (asiatic/oriental/trumpet/tiger) and cover orange, pink, red, white, a solid warm-plus-white spread. The one clear omission is yellow/gold, a staple asiatic and trumpet lily color; noticeable but a single-color gap, so minor rather than notable.
Eustoma color variants: blue, purple, pink, white, pale yellow/apricot, plus picotee bicolors. Lisianthus does not do true red, so this spans its realistic garden range well.

Cultivars of one warm-season grass (Schizachyrium scoparium: 'The Blues', 'Standing Ovation', etc.). The 'orange' is fall foliage/plume color; grasses are shopped by foliage color and form, not flower color, so there is no flower-color range to miss.

The members read as bedding Lobelia erinus color variants (blue/white/pink/purple), which genuinely has no red. But a card simply labeled 'Lobelia' also implies the iconic scarlet cardinal flower (L. cardinalis), a distinct perennial form. Red is the honest absence, kept minor because it belongs to a different species than the bedding types shown.
Russell-hybrid garden lupine color variants; blue/purple/pink/red/white/yellow is essentially the full lupine rainbow.

Form types (star, saucer, southern evergreen, yellow cucumber-tree). Trees are shopped by size/form/evergreen-vs-deciduous, not flower color; white + pink/magenta + yellow spans the realistic magnolia palette (deep-purple moderns like 'Black Tulip' fold into magenta/pink).
Miscanthus sinensis cultivars ('Gracillimus', 'Zebrinus', 'Morning Light'). The pale red/yellow are plume tones; foliage and form are the shopping axis, not flower color.
Red/pink/white/apricot(yellow)/orange covers the full mandevilla/dipladenia tropical-vine range.
White/yellow/pink/apricot/red is the complete Argyranthemum palette.

Both distinct forms (African/erecta, French/patula, signet/tenuifolia) and colors. Marigolds are inherently a warm-tone genus (orange/yellow/red/mahogany) with no blue/pink/purple, so the range is well covered; white is a niche novelty, not an expected gap.
Philadelphus is inherently a white-flowered fragrance shrub (cultivars differ by size and single vs double, some with a purple eye). All-white is correct, not a missing-color gap.
Blue ('Heavenly Blue'), purple, pink, red, white covers the Ipomoea range; slightly mixed since white moonflower (I. alba) is a related species, but color coverage is complete.
Tropaeolum is a warm-tone genus (orange/yellow/red/cream/mahogany) with no blue or purple; orange/yellow/red spans it well.

Physocarpus is shopped by FOLIAGE color (gold 'Dart's Gold', burgundy 'Diabolo', green); the flowers are inherently small white/pale-pink clusters. No flower-color gap; the real color axis is leaves, not captured here.
Chasmanthium latifolium grown for oat-like seed heads and foliage, not flowers; not a flower-color plant, so nothing to miss.

Empty flower-color is expected: ornamental kale/cabbage color lives in the FOLIAGE rosette (white, pink, magenta/purple), and flowering actually means bolting. Members are leaf-color and leaf-form (feather/fringe) variants; the blank flower-color is a data artifact, not a shopper-facing gap.
Cortaderia cultivars (incl. variegated 'Gold Band', pink 'Rosea'); plume colors are white/cream and pink. Grass form/plume is the shopping axis, adequately covered.
Eight colors including near-black (grey) - the most complete bedding-plant palette possible; nothing missing.
Distinct Passiflora species (blue P. caerulea, red P. coccinea/racemosa, purple, pink, white). Grouped by species/form but the color range is complete.
Red/pink/purple(lavender)/white is the full Pentas lanceolata palette; it has no true blue, yellow, or orange, so nothing realistic is missing.
Herbaceous, tree, and Itoh forms plus colors; white/pink/red/coral(orange)/yellow (Itoh & tree) covers everything peonies do. No blue or purple exists in peonies, so the range is complete.
Very broad range including true blue and yellow; the near-black novelties fold into purple. Full coverage.
Conifers (dwarf mugo, white pine, Japanese black pine) with no ornamental flowers; the color axis is N/A, so the empty color set is correct.
Yellow/orange/red/pink/magenta/white is the full moss-rose (Portulaca grandiflora) bright palette; complete.

Reads as shrubby cinquefoil (Potentilla fruticosa) color cultivars: yellow is the classic, with newer white ('Abbotswood'), orange/red ('Red Ace'), and pink ('Pink Beauty') forms. Orange, pink, red, white, yellow spans the full realistic shrub-potentilla palette. A few herbaceous pink/red species may also be folded in, so it leans slightly mixed, but color coverage is complete.

Persian buttercup (Ranunculus asiaticus) is a florist plant sold in nearly every hue. Orange, pale pink, pale yellow, pink, purple, red, white, yellow is a broad, well-rounded spread; no shopper-relevant gap.

The 10 members are Cercis cultivars differentiated by FOLIAGE and habit (purple-leaf 'Forest Pansy', gold 'Rising Sun', weeping 'Ruby Falls'), not by bloom color. Redbud flowers are genuinely only magenta-pink through pink with a white ('Alba') form, so the flower range is already complete.

Foliage plant: rex begonias are grown for patterned leaves, not flowers, so the empty flower-color set is correct. Members are distinct foliage cultivars/forms. Flower-color coverage is not the shopping axis here.

A mix of evergreen rhododendrons AND azaleas (the presence of yellow signals deciduous azaleas are included), plus color variants. Eight colors give excellent coverage, but deciduous azaleas are famous for flame/coral-orange ('Gibraltar', Exbury hybrids) and no member provides orange, so a shopper hunting that classic azalea color comes up empty.

Hibiscus syriacus cultivars in different colors (some also single vs double form). Blue/lavender, magenta, pink, purple, and white are the true rose-of-Sharon range; there is no genuine yellow or true red in this species, so the palette is effectively complete.

Spans multiple species (perennial S. nemorosa/sylvestris in blue/purple/pink/white). The set is entirely cool-toned and MISSING RED, yet scarlet salvia (S. splendens bedding, S. coccinea, pineapple sage) is one of the most iconic salvias, and S. greggii/microphylla add coral, salmon, and hot warm tones. A shopper wanting red salvia would clearly be frustrated.

Different squill species/forms (S. siberica, its 'Alba' white form, S. mischtschenkoana, S. peruviana). Scilla is fundamentally a blue bulb with a white variant; blue plus white covers essentially the whole realistic range (violet reads within blue; pink forms are rare).

The stored colors (pale pink, pink, red) are entirely the tall border-sedum / Hylotelephium 'Autumn Joy' side. The whole creeping-stonecrop half of the genus is missing its signature bloom colors: bright yellow (S. acre, S. kamtschaticum) and white (S. album). A shopper wanting a yellow-flowering groundcover sedum finds none.

The 7 members are Amelanchier species/cultivars (canadensis, laevis, x grandiflora 'Autumn Brilliance', alnifolia) differing by size, habit, and fall color, not flower color. Serviceberry bloom is uniformly white, so white is the complete range.

Leucanthemum x superbum cultivars differentiated mainly by flower FORM (single 'Becky', double 'Wirral Supreme', frilly, quilled) with a few soft-yellow selections ('Banana Cream', 'Broadway Lights'). White plus pale yellow is the complete shasta-daisy range.

Landscape rose series (Knock Out, Drift, etc.) are sold across a wide palette, but the members only cover pink and red. Missing the very common yellow ('Sunny Knock Out'), white, and apricot/coral/peach (Drift, Oso Easy) selections a shopper of low-care landscape roses would absolutely expect.

Cotinus cultivars chosen for FOLIAGE (purple 'Royal Purple'/'Grace' vs gold 'Golden Spirit' vs green) plus the smoky flower plumes. The plume colors (magenta-pink, pale orange, pale red, pink) reasonably span the hazy 'smoke' range; flower color is not the real selection axis, so no meaningful gap.

Antirrhinum cultivars across a broad palette. Red, pink, orange, yellow, and white are covered, but snapdragons also come in deep purple/violet-burgundy ('Black Prince', 'Night and Day') that always appear in rainbow mixes and no member provides. Modest gap, main range well covered.
Galanthus species/cultivars (nivalis, elwesii, double 'Flore Pleno') differing by size and form. Snowdrops are always white with green markings, so white is the complete and only realistic color.
Leucojum species/forms (spring L. vernum vs summer L. aestivum) differing by season/size. Uniformly white with green-yellow tips; white is the complete range.

Two distinct forms grouped: white bridal-wreath spireas (S. x vanhouttei, prunifolia, nipponica) and pink/rose Japanese spireas (S. japonica 'Anthony Waterer', 'Magic Carpet'). White plus pink/rose is the genuine spirea flower range (the deepest japonicas read as carmine-rose, still within pink).

Conifer: the 10 members are Picea species/forms selected for foliage color and habit (blue spruce, Norway, dwarf Alberta, weeping). No flowers, so the empty color set is correct and flower-color coverage does not apply.

Different Ornithogalum species: the common white spring bulb (O. umbellatum, thyrsoides, arabicum) plus the orange pot species O. dubium. O. dubium also comes in a bright yellow that isn't represented; a minor gap since white and orange already cover the two main forms.

Limonium sinuatum color strains, the classic dried flower. Blue/purple, orange/apricot, pink, white, and yellow give a strong spread across the real statice range (lavender/violet reads within blue). No shopper-relevant gap.
Matthiola color forms of the fragrant spring annual. Magenta, pale yellow/cream, pink, purple/lavender, red, and white cover the full stock palette comprehensively.
Xerochrysum/Helichrysum bracteatum color forms. Orange, pink, purple, red, white, and yellow span the warm-and-bright strawflower range thoroughly; no meaningful gap.
Clethra alnifolia cultivars only occur in white and blush-pink ('Ruby Spice'/'Hummingbird'); both present, so the range is complete.
Annual Helianthus cultivars in yellow, pale-yellow, orange and red - the core garden range; cream/white types are niche and mahogany bicolors read as red.
White, purple, pink and apricot(orange) span essentially the full Lobularia palette.
Red, pink, purple, blue and white cover the classic Lathyrus odoratus range (cream reads within white).
Ipomoea batatas is grown for FOLIAGE (lime/chartreuse, near-black, bronze, variegated), not flowers; the empty flower-color list is correct, and members are distinct leaf-color selections.
Panicum virgatum cultivars differ in foliage/form; the orange/red are fall foliage tones, not blooms - flower color is not the axis for an ornamental grass.
Hibiscus rosa-sinensis spans a full warm-to-white rainbow (red, orange, yellow, pink, magenta, purple, white); excellent coverage.
Orange, red and yellow ('Flava') are the entire Campsis color range.
Warm colors (red, orange, yellow, pink, apricot) plus white span the tuberhybrida range; no blue or purple exists in this plant.
Very broad color span (red, yellow, orange, pink, purple, white plus near-black purple and green/parrot novelties) across many flower forms; color is thoroughly covered.
Mix of trailing annual hybrids and species like V. bonariensis and V. rigida; colors well spread across red, pink, magenta, purple, blue and white.
Spike speedwell only comes in blue/violet, purple, pink and white - all present; it does not occur in red/orange/yellow, so the range is complete.

Seven distinct species grouped by genus (snowball, doublefile, Koreanspice, arrowwood, etc.); viburnums are essentially white-flowered (a few open from pink buds), so all-white is botanically accurate, not a shopper gap - they differ by form, fragrance, fruit and fall color, not flower hue.
Annual vinca (Catharanthus roseus) in white, pink, red, purple and apricot(orange), often with contrasting eyes - covers the range well.
Flower colors pink, red and white cover the Weigela florida flower range; several members are dark-leaf or variegated foliage forms, but no additional flower colors are missing.

Anemone genus spans spring Grecian (blanda), fall Japanese, and poppy/De Caen anemones; the set has blue, pink, magenta and white but misses the vivid RED and true purple the iconic poppy anemone is known for.

Ilex verticillata is grown for BERRIES not flowers; red plus orange berry cultivars meet shopper expectation (gold-berried 'Winter Gold' is niche). Members are berry-color/pollinator selections of one species.
Blue/violet, purple-magenta, pink, white and yellow span the Torenia fournieri range, typically with bicolor throats.
Chinese, Japanese and American species; purple/violet, blue-purple, pink ('Rosea') and white ('Alba') cover the full flower range.
Species and x intermedia hybrids in yellow, orange ('Jelena') and red ('Diane') - the complete Hamamelis range.
White, yellow, red, pink and orange span the modern Achillea hybrid palette (magenta/lilac forms read within pink/red).
Full rainbow including lime-green ('Envy'); Zinnia does not produce true blue/purple, so coverage is essentially complete.
